title: Sunk Cost Trap

content.statement: Past investment should not drive future decisions; only future expected value should.
content.question: If you had not yet invested in this, would you start it today knowing what you know now?
content.equation: Continue If: Expected Future Value > Future Cost (ignoring sunk cost)
content.mental_model: Weight you keep dragging because you already paid for it.
content.business_lens: In project reviews, ask "if we had not invested anything yet, would we start this today?"
content.life_lens: Stop justifying staying in something because of how much you already gave; decide based on what is ahead.
sop: Define the future cost to continue
Compare without referencing what was spent
If expected value is less than cost, kill or pivot
Reallocate freed resources to higher-value work
content.explainer: Companies pour money into failing projects because executives feel responsible for prior decisions. The fix is structural: pre-commit to kill criteria; conduct sunk-cost-blind reviews; reward people for stopping bad projects.
questions: If you had not yet invested in this, would you start it today knowing what you know now?
Which current project have you defended using the phrase 'we've already put so much into this'? That phrase is the tell.
If a completely new operator took over your business tomorrow with zero attachment to past decisions, what's the first thing they'd cut?

definitions.def: Money or time already spent that can't be recovered.

content.equation_example: Future cost to continue: $20,000. Expected future value: $35,000, ignoring the $80,000 already spent. $35,000 is greater than $20,000, so continuing is justified on its own merits regardless of the $80,000 sunk. If future cost had exceeded future value, the correct call is to stop, no matter how much was already spent.
content.closing_statement: What you already spent has no vote in this.

caption:
Principle Nº 297: Sunk Cost Trap

Past investment should not drive future decisions; only future expected value should.

Ask yourself: If you had not yet invested in this, would you start it today knowing what you know now?
